body_txt: Video Games tend to standardize their control schemes. Especially when there was just a joystick and four buttons, there wasn't much option to play around with the system. But there's no guarantee that a developer will use what's usual, and might do something very different instead. This difference will completely throw you off your game, as if your instinct is one button is used to jump, crouching is the worst thing you could do. This can also transfer between different games—especially if they are in the same or a similar genre. Any game where you can change the control scheme will obviously avoid this by default, though the effectiveness depends on how far the game will let you remap its controls. PC games generally let the user reassign each button but consoles have also evolved to offer different layouts (thumbstick direction, regular or inverted, can be a hotly discussed preference). Emulators, special controllers, and 3rd-party utilities can function as a workaround of sorts as well. Of course, allowing you to change the control scheme in a game with different characters who need different schemes can lead to this within a single game. Even outside of video games this can apply to tabletop games, athletics and even regular occupations. Assuming a Support Party Member is easily translated from one RPG to another may lead to taking risks that are not advised . When All You Have Is a Hammer… may be less effective switching from a ball-peen hammer to a rubber mallet. And not everyone has a Universal Driver's License . Reflexive Response is when someone makes a (seemingly logical) decision without fully realizing what they are doing. Psychologists call this negative transfer. And, on a neurological note, it's technically not your muscles at fault: Dumb Muscle is just that, an unthinking hunk of contractile, fibrous flesh attached to some bones. The thing that's really to blame is the cerebellum, a knot of tightly-folded tissue at the base of the brain responsible for conditioning of physical responses, note it's the reason you don't have to think, "lift right foot, adjust shoulders to maintain balance, extend right leg, swing left arm to balance pace, shift weight to right foot using hips, lift left leg..." every time you want to walk but "cerebellar memory" doesn't roll off the tongue as nicely. For when this is done intentionally as a game effect, see Interface Screw . This can go beyond gaming, as examples below show. Any control system for a device that can be easily confused for another falls into it. This is why we have Stock Control Settings . Compare Caps Lock, Num Lock, Missiles Lock , Centipede's Dilemma , and Stumbling in the New Form , contrast Noob Bridge (which occurs when a game's control scheme has an extra aspect that's non-standard) and see also Wikipedia Behavior . May result in complaints of They Changed It, Now It Sucks! . May also come into play in a Wax On, Wax Off situation.
